name: Palaemon
description: Palaemon is a minor sea god in Greek mythology, originally known as Melicertes, the son of Ino and Athamas. His story is marked by tragedy and transformation. Driven mad by Hera, Athamas killed his son Learchus, and Ino, to escape his wrath, leaped into the sea with Melicertes. Transformed into sea deities by Poseidon, Ino became Leucothea, a benevolent sea goddess, and Melicertes became Palaemon. He is a protector of sailors in distress, often appearing to them in times of peril to offer assistance. Palaemon is particularly associated with the Isthmian Games, which were established in his honor by Sisyphus. He is considered a helper of those who are shipwrecked and generally a friend of those at sea. His worship was centered at the Isthmus of Corinth, where a sanctuary was dedicated to him. While not a major Olympian deity, Palaemon held a significant role in the lives of those who depended on the sea for their livelihood and survival. He represents the hope and salvation found even in the most desperate situations, a testament to the transformative power of the sea.
subtitle: Sea god, protector of sailors

keyMyths.title: The Leap of Ino
keyMyths.description: Ino, fleeing from her crazed husband Athamas, leaps into the sea with her son Melicertes, both transforming into sea deities.

keyMyths.title: Palaemon's Protection
keyMyths.description: Palaemon is known for aiding sailors in distress, often appearing to those shipwrecked or facing perilous seas.

keyMyths.title: The Isthmian Games
keyMyths.description: The Isthmian Games were established by Sisyphus in honor of Palaemon, held at the Isthmus of Corinth as a major athletic festival.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A narrative poem recounting myths and legends, including the transformation of Ino and Melicertes.
sources.text: A narrative poem recounting myths and legends, including the transformation of Ino and Melicertes.

sources.author: Apollonius Rhodius
sources.description: An epic poem about Jason and the Argonauts, referencing Palaemon as a protector of sailors.
sources.text: An epic poem about Jason and the Argonauts, referencing Palaemon as a protector of sailors.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: A travelogue describing ancient Greece, including the Isthmian Games and Palaemon's sanctuary.
